caveats: a: OEWS combines crude petroleum and natural gas extraction and includes employers of all sizes.
a: Technology sources establish capability and efficiency, not a measured labor-substitution share.
rho: Production, energy, and emissions improvements are not all labor substitution.
rho: Appalachian shale, associated gas, conventional mature fields, coalbed methane, and liquids-rich plays have different implementation economics.
e: Establishments are not firms, and one producer may own many properties or establishments.
e: The 43.21% sector margin bridge is highly sensitive to gas prices, basis, hedging, depletion, gathering charges, and development spending and may materially misclassify firms.
s5: EIA deal value is not a firm-count transfer rate and is dominated by large transactions.
s5: The SBA source is cross-industry and does not measure completed control transfers.
q: Large public producers have marketing, takeaway, hedging, and scale options unlike many LMM firms.
q: Gas-price and basis moves can swamp automation savings even when the saving itself is retained.
d5: AEO cases are scenarios, and LNG, pipeline, power, and regulatory assumptions can change materially.
d5: Rising national demand does not guarantee growth for mature, takeaway-constrained, or high-cost LMM properties.
o: Production may consolidate into larger operators without eliminating the operator requirement.
o: Contract operations and midstream integration can change who performs tasks but not the need for an accountable producer.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring sales by volume and energy content to marketers, pipelines, utilities, processors, industrial buyers, power generators, and LNG-linked channels, generally priced from Henry Hub or regional indexes adjusted for basis, energy content, gathering, processing, transport, hedges, and contract terms; some operators also earn contract operating fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Passive royalty and mineral interests, non-operating financing vehicles, shell leaseholders, gas-field service contractors classified elsewhere, midstream pipelines and processing businesses outside the code, oil-dominant producers, captive units, and businesses without transferable control.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. operating businesses repeatedly exploring for, developing, producing, treating, measuring, and selling natural gas and field-recovered hydrocarbon liquids to external purchasers, including transferable operators working wells for others.

a.bridge: BLS measures combined oil and gas extraction rather than natural-gas-only operators, showing 13.62% management, 9.28% business and financial, 15.79% extraction, 6.53% maintenance, and 5.88% production occupations, plus engineering, science, office, and logistics. EIA and DOE document AI, automated drilling, well-performance prediction, and predictive maintenance in oil and gas. I translate this combined mix and capability evidence into wage-weighted remaining exposure; existing automation and gas-field differences limit precision.
caveats: OEWS combines crude petroleum and natural gas extraction and includes employers of all sizes.
Technology sources establish capability and efficiency, not a measured labor-substitution share.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-wage study of independent natural gas producers separating current automation from remaining geoscience, engineering, production, compression, maintenance, land, accounting, marketing, compliance, and administrative hours would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can improve geology and reservoir interpretation, well-performance and decline prediction, drilling and completion planning, production and compression optimization, predictive maintenance, leak and methane anomaly detection, scheduling, lease accounting, regulatory reporting, procurement, and administration. Field maintenance, intervention, emergency response, safety, inspection, and accountable control remain physical or human-led.

d5.bridge: EIA forecasts U.S. net natural gas exports rising 18% to 18.7 Bcf/d in 2026 and another 10% to 20.5 Bcf/d in 2027 as LNG and Mexico pipeline demand grow. AEO2026 identifies LNG as the fastest-growing demand source, supporting production growth and export capacity reaching 27.7 Bcf/d by 2030. I convert national demand and production drivers into a five-year constant-quality ratio for current extraction output. National growth may bypass constrained LMM basins, while associated gas and large low-cost producers may capture a disproportionate share.
caveats: AEO cases are scenarios, and LNG, pipeline, power, and regulatory assumptions can change materially.
Rising national demand does not guarantee growth for mature, takeaway-constrained, or high-cost LMM properties.
d5.change_evidence: EIA AEO annual production and demand tables through 2031 by basin and end use, combined with eligible firms' reserve reports, decline curves, gathering and transport commitments, LNG exposure, and development budgets, would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: LNG exports, Mexico pipelines, power generation, data centers, manufacturing, petrochemicals, and coal displacement support demand. Renewable generation, efficiency, pipeline constraints, local basis collapse, price elasticity, regulation, methane costs, and associated-gas competition create downside.

caveats: Establishments are not firms, and one producer may own many properties or establishments.
The 43.21% sector margin bridge is highly sensitive to gas prices, basis, hedging, depletion, gathering charges, and development spending and may materially misclassify firms.
e.change_evidence: A named review of the 165 candidates covering ultimate ownership, operated production, gas and liquids mix, recurring external sales, cycle-normalized EBITDA, reserves, gathering and firm-transport commitments, hedges, plugging and methane liabilities, customer concentration, and transferable control would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Census reports 1,342 employer establishments and the frozen estimate identifies 165 firms in the EBITDA band, supporting a meaningful independent-producer universe. Passive interests, non-operated properties, large public groups, oil-dominant firms, distressed or shut-in assets, gathering-heavy businesses, contract operators without transferable production, and volatile normalized gas EBITDA reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 211130 ($6,822,429,368) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 211130 ($184,612,917,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.4321 (margins.json[211]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Oil/Gas (Production and Exploration) EBITDA/Sales 43.21%); revenue/employee $7,041,188 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 211130); class <5: 521 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,308,232 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $565,287 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 97 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,208,526 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,682,704 -> IN $1-10M band; class 10-19: 68 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,575,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,297,985 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $60,162,810 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $25,996,350 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $524,577,837 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $226,670,083 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,119,817,540 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,348,073,159 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 165

caveats: Production may consolidate into larger operators without eliminating the operator requirement.
Contract operations and midstream integration can change who performs tasks but not the need for an accountable producer.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of regulators accepting autonomous systems as accountable well operators, or widespread owner self-operation eliminating independent operating businesses, would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Every remaining unit of gas requires a legally and operationally accountable entity to hold rights, supervise wells and contractors, manage compression and measurement, meet safety and environmental duties, nominate and market production, and close or plug assets. AI can reduce staffing but cannot become the regulated operator within five years.

q.bridge: EQT and TXO report that natural gas prices are tied to Henry Hub and regional indexes, with substantial basis, quality, gathering, and transport adjustments and volatility outside producer control. I translate these public-producer disclosures to LMM operators. A price-taking producer's internal labor efficiency generally does not directly lower the benchmark, so much of the saving can initially remain with the operator; over five years, competitive drilling, purchaser and midstream renegotiation, service inflation, and lower economic cutoffs erode it.
caveats: Large public producers have marketing, takeaway, hedging, and scale options unlike many LMM firms.
Gas-price and basis moves can swamp automation savings even when the saving itself is retained.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm gas purchase, gathering, processing, and transport agreements; basis and hedge histories; royalty and tax burdens; lease operating costs; and margin persistence after verified automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Index-linked sales provide little automatic contractual pass-through of internal savings. Retention is reduced by local basis pressure, firm transport and gathering costs, royalty and tax burdens, acreage and service competition, curtailments, and sustaining capital.

caveats: Production, energy, and emissions improvements are not all labor substitution.
Appalachian shale, associated gas, conventional mature fields, coalbed methane, and liquids-rich plays have different implementation economics.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at independent U.S. gas producers reporting model accuracy, overrides, downtime, compression energy, production, leak and emissions outcomes, maintenance avoidance, and labor hours saved would narrow the estimate.
rho.rationale: SCADA, well, compression, flow, pressure, maintenance, geological, market, and accounting data support implementation. Heterogeneous legacy wells, remote connectivity, cybersecurity, measurement and emissions compliance, sparse failures, high-consequence controls, contractor interfaces, and limited LMM data teams constrain operational deployment.

s5.bridge: EIA reports $234 billion of U.S. oil and gas E&P merger and acquisition spending in 2023, the highest real value since 2012, but the value was dominated by large consolidation. SBA reports broad family-business succession pressure. I translate these different populations into a five-year LMM control-transfer probability, discounting megadeals and recognizing that acreage, working-interest, and royalty trades need not transfer an eligible firm. Basin consolidation may make the succession proxy understate strategic sales.
caveats: EIA deal value is not a firm-count transfer rate and is dominated by large transactions.
The SBA source is cross-industry and does not measure completed control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Enverus, Rystad, PitchBook, state-record, and basin transaction data matched to eligible private 211130 firms, distinguishing entity control transfers from acreage and interest sales, would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Commodity cycles, owner succession, scale economies, reserve replacement, infrastructure commitments, and buyers seeking contiguous acreage support transfers. Basis exposure, reserve uncertainty, plugging and methane liabilities, hedges, gathering contracts, joint interests, and preference for asset deals can prevent qualifying control transfers.

principal_driver: Growing LNG and power demand combines with sensor, well, compression, market, and maintenance data to support valuable AI-assisted operations.
principal_weakness: Labor is a tiny share of receipts, while basis, takeaway, commodity prices, and reservoir performance overwhelm the economic effect of task automation.
